text: The surgeon uses a 12mm peri-umbilical optical trocar and two 5mm trocars to explore the abdomen and inguinal fossae. On the right side, a direct and an indirect hernia are identified, with the indirect hernia already reduced. The peritoneum is opened, and the right direct hernia defect is exposed. On the left side, adhesions between the sigmoid colon and abdominal wall are sharply dissected, the peritoneum is opened, and the left direct hernia defect is revealed.

The surgeon performs medial dissection in the retropubic space, creating a retropubic tunnel. A rolled-up polypropylene mesh is introduced through the 12mm optical trocar and grasped with laparoscopic forceps. The mesh is passed from the left side through the retropubic tunnel to the right side using a reabsorbable stay suture. The mesh is then deployed and oriented with reabsorbable stitches of varying lengths used as landmarks.

The surgeon secures the bat-shaped mesh in place by placing tacks on both sides and at the center of the prosthesis, ensuring it covers all hernia defects. The retropubic portion of the mesh aids in preventing dislocation. The peritoneum is then closed with a running barbed reabsorbable suture.
